result:
### The Orchard of Unsent Messages

At the edge of the city, past the last bus stop and the warehouse with the broken blue windows, there was an orchard where unsent messages grew on trees.

No one agreed on when it had started. Some said it appeared after the first telephone wires were strung across the rooftops. Others insisted it was older, that before there were phones or letters or even paper, people had still carried words inside them until the words grew heavy enough to fall into the soil.

The orchard was not large. A low stone wall surrounded it, mottled with moss and old gum. Inside were perhaps forty trees, each different. Some were thin and silver-barked, trembling in the wind. Some were squat and stubborn, with roots that lifted the paths. In spring they flowered in pale colors nobody could name. In autumn, their fruit ripened.

The fruit looked like ordinary fruit at first glance—pears, plums, apples, figs—but each one contained a message that had never been sent.

People came quietly.

They arrived with hands in pockets, collars turned up, sunglasses on cloudy days. They came after divorces, after funerals, before weddings, during lunch breaks. Teenagers came at dusk and dared one another to take bites. Old women came with shopping bags and sat for hours beneath the branches without picking anything.

There was no sign at the gate, only a brass bell with a cracked handle. If you rang it, the keeper would appear.

Her name was Mara. She wore green boots in all seasons and carried pruning shears in a leather holster. She had been the keeper for twenty-three years, though she looked neither young nor old, but weathered in the way of good wood.

“The rules are simple,” she would say to visitors. “You may pick only what bends toward your hand. You may taste, but you cannot unknow. You may not take fruit from another person’s tree unless invited. And you must leave before dark.”

“Why before dark?” people always asked.

Mara always answered the same way: “At night, the orchard listens back.”

Most people laughed nervously.

No one stayed to test it.

The trees were not labeled, but somehow people found what they had come for. A man who had once typed *I’m sorry I didn’t believe you* and deleted it would find himself beneath a pear tree leaning over the north path. A girl who had written *Please don’t go* but pressed nothing would discover tiny red plums falling at her feet. A mother who had never said *I am proud of you* because pride felt dangerous would stand trembling before a tree full of golden apples.

The taste varied. Apologies were often bitter at first, then sweet near the core. Confessions could be sharp enough to make the eyes water. Love notes were unpredictable: honeyed, salty, sour, smoky. Lies never ripened properly. They stayed hard and green, and if bitten, filled the mouth with chalk.

One October afternoon, a man named Elias came to the orchard.

He was not old, though grief had made an early winter of his face. His father had died three weeks earlier, and since then Elias had been sorting through drawers, tools, unpaid bills, batteries, eyeglasses, keys to unknown locks. In a kitchen cabinet, behind a stack of chipped mugs, he had found his father’s old phone.

It still held a charge.

There were no messages of importance on it, only reminders, missed calls, a recipe for lentil soup, photographs of receipts. But in the drafts folder, Elias found one unsent text addressed to him.

It said only: *Eli, I should have told you—*

Nothing more.

The dash at the end became a splinter in his mind.

So he came to the orchard, though he did not believe in it.

Mara met him at the gate.

“I’m looking for something unfinished,” he said.

“They usually are,” Mara replied.

He expected her to ask for a name, a date, some proof of sorrow. Instead she opened the gate and stepped aside.

The orchard smelled of wet leaves and fermentation. Bees moved slowly in the cold air. Somewhere, fruit dropped with a soft thud.

Elias walked the paths for nearly an hour. Trees rustled as he passed, but none bent toward him. He began to feel foolish. Of course there was no fruit containing his father’s message. Of course there was no remedy for a sentence broken in half.

He was turning to leave when he noticed a tree near the wall.

It was small, almost hidden behind a larger one. Its bark was dark and ridged. Its branches hung low, crowded with dull brown fruit shaped like figs. One branch extended as he approached, not dramatically, not like magic in stories, but with the slight courtesy of someone moving a chair closer.

Elias reached out.

The fruit came away easily.

It was warm in his palm.

He looked back toward the gate. Mara stood far off, watching without expression.

Elias bit into the fruit.

At once he was in his father’s truck.

Not physically. He knew he was still in the orchard, with juice running down his hand. But also he was eight years old again, sitting in the passenger seat, knees dusty from soccer practice. His father was driving with one hand, the other tapping the steering wheel. The radio was full of static. They were not speaking, but it was not an unhappy silence.

Then the scene shifted.

Elias was sixteen, slamming a door. His father stood in the hallway holding a laundry basket, looking startled, then tired. Words hovered between them like insects neither knew how to swat away.

Then twenty-two. A train platform. His father saying, “Call when you get there,” and Elias saying, “Yeah,” already looking elsewhere.

Then thirty-one. A hospital corridor. His father asleep under a thin blanket, mouth open slightly, smaller than he had ever been.

The fruit tasted of smoke, black tea, and something like rain on concrete.

A voice came—not in his ears, exactly, but in the place where memory pretends to be sound.

*Eli, I should have told you I was scared too.*

That was all.

No explanation. No hidden inheritance. No confession of a second family, no secret illness, no grand revelation that would reorder the past.

Just that.

*I was scared too.*

Elias stood very still.

He had imagined many endings to the draft. *I should have told you I loved you.* *I should have told you about your mother.* *I should have told you I was sorry.* But not this. Never this small, ordinary bridge across the years.

He finished the fruit slowly, core and all.

When he returned to the gate, Mara handed him a cloth for his hands.

“Did it help?” she asked.

Elias considered lying. Then he considered telling the truth and discovered he did not yet know what the truth was.

“It changed the shape of the question,” he said.

Mara nodded, as if this was the most one could reasonably expect.

Before he left, Elias turned back to the orchard. The little tree by the wall had fewer fruit now, but its branches seemed lighter.

“What happens to the messages after someone hears them?” he asked.

“They become compost,” Mara said. “If they’re ready.”

“And if they aren’t?”

“They grow again next year.”

Elias walked home through the city with the smell of leaves in his coat.

That night, he opened his own phone. There were drafts there too, of course. Everyone has them. A note to an old friend. An apology to his sister. Three words to someone he had loved and let drift away because answering fully had felt too dangerous at the time.

He did not send them all.

But he sent one.

Outside, beyond the last bus stop, the orchard settled into darkness. Its trees lifted their branches to the night. The wind moved through them gently, and if anyone had been foolish enough to remain past dusk, they might have heard it:

The soft murmur of everything almost said, waiting for a hand, a season, a mouth brave enough to taste.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
